summary: I moved to Charlotte in 1958 to work for The Charlotte Observer as a reporter. During those years, I covered city and county government, education, and growth problems extensively. Charlotte was undergoing significant changes, including annexation in 1957 and the beginnings of urban renewal downtown. I witnessed the city's efforts to rebuild and modernize, including zoning challenges and financial concerns, all of which shaped the political landscape leading up to the consolidation vote.

I worked at The Charlotte Observer starting in 1958 as a reporter and later became city editor from 1960 to 1965. The paper assigned me to cover city government, education, desegregation, and growth issues. I also moved to the editorial page in 1965, which gave me more freedom to engage with community committees and advocate for civic improvements. The Observer played a crucial role in informing the public about Charlotte’s political and social developments during those transformative years.

I read Dr. Schley Lyons' book about Charlotte politics and consolidation, which helped me understand the broader context of the city's political changes. His work provided valuable historical insight into the period from the 1957 annexation through the consolidation vote. Although I didn't work directly with him, his scholarship was a useful resource in framing the political environment I reported on and participated in.

Mr. Wicker compiled a compendium book about Charlotte’s political and civic history, which I reviewed to deepen my understanding of the consolidation issue. His work, alongside Dr. Lyons', provided helpful background as I covered the city's growth and political debates. While I didn’t collaborate with him personally, his documentation was a valuable reference during my reporting and editorial work.

In the late 1950s and early 1960s, the Chamber of Commerce was the dominant civic organization in Charlotte, often more influential than the mayor’s office. Leading businessmen who wanted to be outstanding citizens sought to be Chamber president, reflecting its power in shaping city policies. Over time, however, the Chamber’s influence waned, and it no longer holds the same leadership role in the community as it did during that era.

Charlotte is located in North Carolina, and much of the city’s zoning and urban development followed patterns common across the state, especially after World War II. The state’s cities, including Charlotte, went through initial rounds of zoning ordinances post-war, which shaped growth and land use. North Carolina’s political and economic context influenced the challenges Charlotte faced with annexation, urban renewal, and consolidation during the late 1950s and 1960s.

After World War II, many North Carolina cities, including Charlotte, implemented their first zoning ordinances, which were quite basic and allowed mixed uses within zones. This period set the stage for the urban growth and renewal challenges Charlotte faced in the 1950s and 1960s. The war’s aftermath influenced the city’s infrastructure and planning efforts, as well as the financial pressures that emerged later.

I was involved with the Pacing Progress Committee in the early 1960s, which focused on financial planning and capital outlay for Charlotte. Herbert M. Wayne, president of North Carolina National Bank, chaired the committee. We advocated for a 'pay as you go' approach, and for several years, the city invested over a million dollars in cash capital projects. This committee exemplified the collaboration between business leaders and civic planners to address Charlotte’s growth challenges.

Herbert M. Wayne was the president of North Carolina National Bank and chaired the Pacing Progress Committee in the early 1960s. He was a friend and influential civic leader who helped guide Charlotte’s financial and urban planning efforts. After his passing, a memorial garden was dedicated to him near the bank’s branch on North Tryon Street, just below Golden Eagle South. His leadership symbolized the close ties between Charlotte’s business community and its urban development.
cues: president of North Carolina National Bank

summary: The North Carolina National Bank was a major financial institution in Charlotte during the late 1950s and 1960s. Its president, Herbert M. Wayne, played a key role in civic committees like the Pacing Progress Committee. The bank’s branch on North Tryon Street near Golden Eagle South became a landmark, especially with the dedication of the Herbert M. Wayne Memorial Garden. The bank symbolized the involvement of Charlotte’s business sector in urban renewal and city planning.

North Tryon Street was a central location in Charlotte where the North Carolina National Bank had a branch office near Golden Eagle South. This area included the Herbert M. Wayne Memorial Garden, which was the first physical redevelopment of a central city property initiated by a private business. North Tryon Street was part of the urban renewal efforts and symbolized the city’s evolving downtown landscape during the 1960s.

Golden Eagle South is a landmark near the North Carolina National Bank branch on North Tryon Street in Charlotte. It is notable because the Herbert M. Wayne Memorial Garden is located just below it, marking one of the first private efforts to improve the central city’s appearance. This area was part of the broader urban renewal and revitalization initiatives in Charlotte during the 1960s.

The Charter Commission formed in 1969 was a pivotal moment in Charlotte’s political history, as it gave me a chance to shift from reporting to active civic engagement. I saw it as an opportunity to implement ideas we had advocated editorially, particularly around consolidation and city governance. The Commission worked on proposals that influenced the eventual consolidation vote and reshaped Charlotte’s governmental structure.

The Central Charlotte Committee was involved in planning the revitalization of downtown Charlotte in the early 1960s. Although I was not an official member, I participated as a sort of recorder and observer, working alongside people like Herbert M. Wayne. The committee produced a scale model designed by architect A.G. Odell Jr. and proposed projects like the Civic Center and a stadium near what is now Interstate 77. Their ideas were ahead of their time but laid groundwork for future urban development.

A.G. Odell Jr. was the architect who designed the scale model for the Central Charlotte Committee’s downtown revitalization plan. His work helped visualize the proposals for the central city, including the Civic Center and other major projects. Odell’s designs influenced Charlotte’s urban planning discussions in the 1960s, even though some ideas were initially too advanced for the city to adopt immediately.

The Civic Center was a major project proposed as part of Charlotte’s downtown revitalization efforts in the 1960s. The first vote in 1966 to approve land purchase for the Civic Center failed, but it coincided with a successful bond issue for urban renewal projects. By 1969, a $10 million approval allowed the building of the Civic Center, marking a significant milestone in Charlotte’s urban development. The project was central to debates about the city’s future and consolidation.

Interstate 77 runs near the west side of Charlotte’s downtown area, close to where there was once a proposal for a major stadium by the Central Charlotte Committee. The flat area near the creek by Fourth Street and I-77 was considered for this stadium in the early 1960s. This location was part of broader urban planning discussions tied to revitalizing the central city and improving public facilities.

Southern Railway owned valuable property behind the Charlotte Civic Center, which had long been used as a parking lot. Negotiations with Southern Railway delayed some urban renewal plans, including removing railroad tracks from downtown. Eventually, the railway’s land became prime real estate with high-rise buildings, illustrating the transformation of Charlotte’s central city over time.

The Greenville project was part of Charlotte’s urban renewal efforts in the 1960s, including areas north of East Trade Street. This project involved replacing deteriorated housing with new developments, such as public housing for the elderly on South Boulevard near Pritchard Memorial Church. The Greenville area’s renewal was approved alongside other urban renewal bond issues in the late 1960s, reflecting the city’s commitment to improving neighborhoods.

East Trade Street was a boundary area for some of Charlotte’s urban renewal projects in the 1960s, including the Greenville project. The area north of East Trade Street saw redevelopment efforts that replaced substandard housing with new public housing and other improvements. These projects were part of the city’s broader efforts to revitalize neighborhoods and improve living conditions during that period.

South Boulevard in Charlotte was the site of a small urban renewal project in the 1960s, including new high-rise public housing for the elderly. This development was located near Pritchard Memorial Church and behind the YMCA. The project was part of a bond issue approved by voters alongside other urban renewal initiatives, showing the city’s focus on improving housing options for vulnerable populations.

Pritchard Memorial Church is located near South Boulevard in Charlotte and was close to one of the city’s urban renewal projects in the 1960s. The new high-rise public housing for the elderly was built behind the church and the YMCA. This area exemplified Charlotte’s efforts to revitalize neighborhoods and provide better housing during that era.

John Belk was an exception to the general rule that Charlotte’s power structure was dominated by unelected officials. He was an elected official who held significant influence in the city. His leadership stood out during the period when the Chamber of Commerce was the dominant civic organization, and he played a key role in Charlotte’s political and civic life.

Milton Short was another influential figure in Charlotte’s political scene, with roots in the Mecklenburg Furniture Industries and an old Charlotte family. He was considered part of the city’s power structure, similar to John Belk. At the time of our discussion, he was running for Congress, showing his continued involvement in public service and politics.

Mecklenburg Furniture Industries was a longstanding business in Charlotte, linked to influential families like that of Milton Short. The company represented part of the traditional economic and social fabric of the city. Its legacy contributed to the prominence of families involved in Charlotte’s civic and political leadership during the mid-20th century.
